meta.title: G05 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW X5 G05 engine — B47, B57, B58, N63, S63, S68, PHEV. Real UK costs, failure data, and honest advice on this luxury SUV. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW X5 G05 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned G05 engine typically costs £2,800–£16,000 supply only, depending on engine — the B47 diesel sits at £2,800–£5,000, while the S63 V8 in the X5 M can reach £9,000–£16,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,560. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW X5 G05 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 3.0-litre straight-six (xDrive40i, 2018–present) is the most reliable G05 engine — smooth, powerful, and with a reputation for bulletproof reliability that has made it one of BMW's most dependable modern engines. The B57 diesel six (xDrive30d/xDrive40d) is also a strong performer with proven durability.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW X5 G05?

acceptedAnswer.text: Water pump and cooling system failures on all B-series engines (B58, B57), valve stem seal and turbo coolant line issues on the N63 V8 (xDrive50i/M50i), crank hub and rod bearing issues on the S63/S68 V8s (X5 M/M60i), battery degradation and cooling system issues on the PHEV models (xDrive45e/xDrive50e), and — unrelated to the engine — the occasional electronic gremlins and suspension component wear.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW X5 G05 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G05 is still relatively new, but early indications are positive. The B58 and B57 engines have strong reputations from their use across the BMW range. The N63 V8 has been improved but remains complex. The S63/S68 V8s in the M variants demand meticulous maintenance. The PHEV variants are still unproven long-term. Overall, the G05 appears to be a dependable modern luxury SUV.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW X5 G05 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is inherently problematic, but the N63 V8 (xDrive50i/M50i) is more complex and expensive to maintain than the six-cylinder options — budget accordingly. An S63-powered X5 M without documented crank hub and rod bearing history is also a risky purchase. If you don't need the V8's performance, the B58 is the smarter choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my G05 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Overheating with no obvious external leak (cooling system failure), blue smoke on start-up (N63 valve stem seals), persistent check engine lights, or rough idle are the key warning signs. For PHEV models, reduced electric range or charging issues may indicate battery problems. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW X5 G05 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: On a G05, replacement almost always makes sense. Against a £35,000 G05, a £5,000 B58 recon is 14% of the car's value — well within our 70% BMW threshold. Even against a lower-value example, the G05's high retained value means replacement is financially viable. On a V8 or M variant, the calculus is similar — the car's value justifies the replacement cost.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW X5 G05 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The B58 can exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with proper maintenance. The B57 diesel is similarly durable. The N63 V8 can also reach high mileage but requires meticulous cooling system and valve train maintenance. The S63/S68 V8s are robust but require crank hub and rod bearing preventative work. PHEV battery life is expected to exceed 100,000 miles but will degrade over time.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the G05 and the F15?

acceptedAnswer.text: The G05 is the fourth-generation X5 (2018–present), built on the CLAR platform with a range of B-series and N-series engines, a 48V mild-hybrid system, and more advanced technology. The F15 is the third-generation X5 (2013–2018), built on a previous platform with a range of N-series engines. The G05 is significantly more refined and technologically advanced.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW X5 G05 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible on the engine itself. Tell us your variant (xDrive40i, xDrive30d, M50i, X5 M, etc.) and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW X5 G05 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the fourth-generation X5 G05 — from the B57 diesel six to the B58 petrol six, the N63 V8 and the S68-powered X5 M. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the hot-vee V8 risks that define this generation. Backed by verified specialist data.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW X5 G05 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the fourth-generation X5 — from the four-cylinder diesel to the six-cylinder petrol and diesel units, the V8s and the plug-in hybrid options.

overview.intro: The G05 is the fourth generation of the BMW X5, unveiled online on 6 June 2018 and entering production in August 2018 as the successor to the F15 X5. Built on the Cluster Architecture (CLAR) platform shared with the G11 7 Series and G30 5 Series, the G05 introduced a range of new engines — including the B58 replacing the N55, and the S63-powered X5 M — along with a 48V mild-hybrid system on later models. A facelift (LCI) entered production in March 2023, bringing updated B58, B57 and S68 engines.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW CLAR (Cluster Architecture) • Predecessor: BMW X5 F15 • Successor: Current model — no successor announced • Sibling body styles in this generation: G05 (standard wheelbase), G18 (long-wheelbase, China), F95 (X5 M)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: As a current-generation model (2018–present), the G05 is still establishing its long-term reliability profile — but the B58 engine is already developing a reputation as one of BMW's most dependable modern six-cylinders, while the N63 V8 remains the most complex option.

items.quote: The G05's engine range is modern, efficient and broadly dependable. The B58 3.0-litre straight-six (xDrive40i, 2018–present) is the standout — 329–381 bhp, smooth power delivery, and a reputation for bulletproof reliability that has made it one of BMW's most dependable modern engines. The B57 3.0-litre diesel six (xDrive30d/xDrive40d) is the torque king — 261–352 bhp and up to 700 Nm of effortless pulling power, with mild-hybrid assistance on later models. The N63 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 (xDrive50i/M50i) delivers 456–523 bhp but retains the hot-vee architecture that can lead to heat-related issues on high-mileage examples. The S68 in the M60i (2023–present) replaces the N63 with an updated design, while the S63 in the X5 M offers 591–617 bhp but demands meticulous maintenance. The plug-in hybrids (xDrive45e, xDrive50e) combine the B58 with an electric motor for 389–483 bhp system output. If you're buying a G05, the B58 petrol is the sensible choice; the B57 diesel is for high-mileage drivers; the N63/S63 V8s demand deeper pockets.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Diesel — six-cylinder torque and reliability
Best Overall — the B58 is a modern legend

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve stem seals, turbo coolant lines

economicsRule.title: The G05 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,000–£7,000 B58 recon against a £35,000 G05 sits at 11–20% of the car's value — well within our 70% BMW threshold. Against a £60,000 example, the same recon drops to 7–12% — a routine maintenance decision. On a G05, replacement almost always makes sense. The V8 and PHEV costs are higher but still proportional to the car's value.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G05 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: B58 xDrive40i — simpler than the V8, lower running costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G05 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N63 M50i — hot-vee V8 with expensive heat-related failures.

keyTakeaways.question: Which G05 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: B58 xDrive40i — six-cylinder performance without the V8 bills.

problems.title: B58/B57 Cooling System & Water Pump Failure
problems.affectedModels: G05 xDrive40i (B58), xDrive30d/40d (B57) — all models with B-series engines.
problems.rootCause: BMW's B-series engines use an electric water pump that has a finite service life. The water pump can fail without warning, causing overheating. The cooling system also has plastic components (thermostat housing, coolant pipes) that can become brittle with age and heat cycling. The B58's integrated cooling system is more complex than earlier BMW engines.
problems.recommendation: Replace the water pump proactively at 60,000–80,000 miles on any B58 or B57-powered G05 — a £400–£800 preventative job is far cheaper than a £4,000+ engine replacement. Monitor the temperature gauge and investigate any coolant loss immediately.

cta.label: Compare B58/B57 engine prices for your G05 →

problems.title: N63 Hot-Vee V8 Heat-Related Failures
problems.rootCause: The N63's "hot-vee" design places the turbochargers inside the V of the engine, generating significant heat. This leads to premature failure of valve stem seals (causing blue smoke on start-up), turbo coolant lines, and timing chain stretch. The N63TU3 (M50i, 2020–2023) improved some aspects but retained the hot-vee architecture.
problems.recommendation: If you own an N63-powered G05, budget for valve stem seal replacement as a preventative measure — a £500–£1,200 investment is far cheaper than the £6,500–£11,000 engine replacement that follows a failure. Monitor oil consumption and investigate any blue smoke on start-up immediately.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your G05 M50i →

problems.title: S63/S68 Crank Hub & Rod Bearings (X5 M)
problems.affectedModels: G05 X5 M, X5 M Competition (2020–2023, S63), X5 M Competition LCI (2024–present, S68).
problems.rootCause: The S63's crank hub can spin on the crankshaft, causing timing to slip and potentially catastrophic engine damage. The rod bearings can also wear prematurely, particularly on cars that have been tracked or driven hard. The S68 (2023–present) has addressed some of these issues, but the high-performance V8 remains a complex engine.
problems.recommendation: If you own an X5 M, budget for a crank hub fix and rod bearing replacement as preventative maintenance. A £1,500–£3,500 investment is far cheaper than the £9,000–£15,000 engine replacement that follows a failure. Use the correct oil and warm the engine properly before hard driving.

cta.label: Compare S63/S68 engine prices for your X5 M →

problems.title: PHEV Battery & Cooling System (xDrive45e/xDrive50e)
problems.rootCause: The plug-in hybrid system uses a lithium-ion battery pack that can degrade over time, particularly if regularly fast-charged or exposed to extreme temperatures. The cooling system for the battery and electric motor is more complex than on conventional engines and can develop leaks. The xDrive50e (2023–present) has a larger battery and more powerful electric motor.
problems.recommendation: If you own a PHEV G05, have the battery health checked at every major service. Avoid frequent fast-charging if possible, and keep the battery between 20% and 80% charge for daily use. A £300–£600 preventative check is far cheaper than a £7,000+ battery replacement.

cta.label: Compare PHEV battery prices for your G05 →
